title: Multi-Qubit Systems

theory.introduction: When we combine multiple qubits, we enter the domain of multi-qubit systems. These systems unlock the most powerful quantum phenomena — entanglement, interference, and genuine quantum parallelism. A system of n qubits can represent 2^n states simultaneously, giving quantum computers their exponential power.
theory.start_from_zero: A single qubit has two basis states: |0⟩ and |1⟩. When we have multiple qubits, we combine them using a mathematical operation called the tensor product. For example, two qubits create four basis states: |00⟩, |01⟩, |10⟩, and |11⟩. Three qubits create eight basis states.
theory.why_it_matters: Multi-qubit systems are where quantum computing becomes truly powerful. Entanglement exists only in multi-qubit systems and enables correlations that are impossible in classical systems. Almost all useful quantum algorithms operate on multiple qubits.
theory.core_idea: Multiple qubits are described by a joint state vector in a combined Hilbert space. The joint state is the tensor product of individual qubit states — unless the qubits are entangled, in which case the joint state cannot be separated into individual parts.
key_points: n qubits span a 2^n dimensional state space
State of multiple qubits is described using tensor products
Entangled states cannot be written as a product of individual qubit states
Multi-qubit gates allow qubits to interact
CNOT is the most important entangling gate
Measurement of one qubit can affect outcomes of others

tensor_product.description: The tensor product combines multiple qubit states into one joint state. If qubit A is in state α|0⟩ + β|1⟩ and qubit B is in state γ|0⟩ + δ|1⟩, their combined state contains all possible combinations of both qubits.

multi_qubit_gates.description: Multi-qubit gates operate on two or more qubits simultaneously. These gates allow qubits to interact with each other, enabling conditional operations and entanglement.
multi_qubit_gates.why_important: Without multi-qubit gates, qubits would behave independently and quantum computers would lose their advantage. These gates allow correlations and interference patterns required for algorithms like Grover search and Quantum Fourier Transform.

key_gates.name: CNOT Gate
key_gates.description: Flips the target qubit only when the control qubit is |1⟩.
key_gates.intuition: Acts like a quantum IF statement.
key_gates.importance: Main gate used to create entanglement

key_gates.name: CZ Gate
key_gates.description: Applies a phase flip only when both qubits are |1⟩.
key_gates.intuition: Changes phase of |11⟩ but keeps probabilities same.

key_gates.name: SWAP Gate
key_gates.description: Exchanges the states of two qubits.

multi_qubit_gates.core_idea: Multi-qubit gates create conditional behavior between qubits. This interaction produces correlations not possible in classical systems.

entanglement.description: Entanglement is a uniquely quantum phenomenon where qubits become strongly correlated. The state of one qubit cannot be described independently of the other.
entanglement.creation: Apply Hadamard to qubit 0, then apply CNOT with qubit 0 as control and qubit 1 as target.

cnot_gate.description: The Controlled-NOT gate flips the target qubit only when the control qubit is |1⟩.
cnot_gate.use_case: Used to create entanglement and perform conditional logic

sections.title: Bell States (Maximally Entangled)
content: These represent strongest possible correlations between two qubits

sections.title: Creating Entanglement Step by Step
content: Final state becomes entangled Bell state

examples.title: Creating Bell State
examples.description: Apply Hadamard to first qubit, then CNOT to create entanglement.
examples.insight: Both qubits become correlated. Measuring one determines the other.

examples.title: Independent Superposition
examples.description: Apply Hadamard to each qubit separately.
examples.insight: All states have equal probability but qubits are not entangled.

comparisons.product_state: Can be written as product of individual qubits
comparisons.entangled_state: Cannot be separated into individual states

common_mistakes: Assuming all multi-qubit states are entangled
Thinking entanglement allows communication faster than light
Forgetting that CNOT flips only when control is 1
Thinking 3 qubits store only 3 values instead of 8 amplitudes

try_this: Create Bell state using H and CNOT
Apply H on all 3 qubits to see 8-state superposition
Create GHZ state using 2 CNOT gates
Use CZ gate to change phase of |11⟩
Use SWAP to exchange qubit states

quiz.question: How many basis states exist for 3 qubits?
Which gate is mainly used to create entanglement?
What does SWAP gate do?
quiz.explanation: SWAP interchanges states of two qubits

quiz.question: What happens when CNOT control is |0⟩?
